caption: Fig. 1. Historical development of sensor-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR) models. From classical machine learning with hand-crafted features and shallow classifiers [11] to the rise of deep learning with CNNs and RNNs [112], the field progressed toward a phase focused on transfer and domain generalization (robustness across users, devices, and datasets [122, 129, 144]). More recently, self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches have enabled pretraining on unlabeled sensor data using contrastive or masked objectives [142]. Today, the field is moving toward foundation models, exemplified by large-scale sensor–language alignment [125], emphasizing scalability, generalization, and interpretability.
description2: The figure plots the historical development of sensor-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR) models, showcasing a progression from classical machine learning to deep learning and eventually self-supervised learning approaches.
description3: Four-panel timeline plots the historical development of sensor-based Human Activity Recognition models, progressing from classical machine learning to foundation models.
questions: How has the approach to building Human Activity Recognition models changed over time?
What are the key stages in the evolution of sensor-based model development?

caption: Fig. 2. Foundations and challenges of sensor-based HAR across multiple abstraction levels: signal-, data-, user-, semantic-, and corpus-level factors jointly define the complexity of learning robust and generalizable activity representations.
description2: The diagram illustrates foundations and challenges of sensor-based human activity recognition, considering signal, data, user, semantic, and corpus-level factors that impact learning robust and generalizable activity representations.
description3: A multi-panel plot organizes sensor-based human activity recognition (HAR) challenges across signal, data, user, semantic, and corpus levels.
questions: What factors contribute to the complexity of learning activity representations in sensor-based human activity recognition?
How do different levels of abstraction influence the challenges in developing generalizable activity representations?

caption: Fig. 3. Definition of Foundation Models [149] and its adaptation across Computer Vision [165], Natural Language Processing [114], and Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition (this work).
description2: The figure defines foundation models and their adaptations across computer vision, natural language processing, and sensor-based human activity recognition.
description3: Two-panel diagram presents a taxonomy of foundation models across computer vision, natural language processing, and sensor-based human activity recognition.
questions: What constitutes a foundation model, according to the provided definition?
How do foundation models adapt to different activity and language processing tasks?

caption: Fig. 4. How foundation models address HAR challenges across signal-, data-, user-, semantic-, and corpus-level dimensions.
description2: The diagram illustrates how foundation models tackle Human-AI Collaboration (HAR) challenges across signal, data, user, semantic, and corpus levels.
description3: Two-panel diagram displays foundation models addressing human activity recognition challenges across five dimensions, shown as radiating spokes from a central hub.
questions: What are the main challenges addressed by foundation models in human activity recognition?
How do different dimensions of human activity recognition relate to foundation model approaches?

caption: Fig. 5. Heuristic 1–7 scores of representative works against six HAR–FM criteria. The “Ideal HAR-FM” panel depicts a target profile. Scores (1 = limited evidence to 7 = strong evidence) are judgment-based syntheses from reported results (compared both to the other models in this survey and to an aspirational “ideal” FM-for-HAR reference point) and are intended for qualitative comparison rather than a leaderboard. Representative works: SelfHAR-700k [164], MASTER [177], OneHAR [145], UniMTS [170], IMU2CLIP [105], SensorLM [171], RelCon [156], Chronos HAR Adapters [152], MHARFedLLM [10], NORMWEAR [95], TxP [123], AURA-MFM [99], Time2Lang [116], and LSM [107].
description2: The figure plots the heuristic scores of 12 representative works against six HAR-FM criteria, with scores ranging from 1 to 7.
description3: Four-panel radar chart displays heuristic scores (1-7) for multiple representative works against six HAR–FM criteria, with an "Ideal HAR-FM" target profile.
questions: What main idea or comparison does the figure present?
What overall structure or pattern is shown in the figure?

caption: Fig. 6. Conceptual taxonomy and lifecycle of foundation models for sensor-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR). This framework synthesizes the diverse methodological patterns into four–phase workflow: By aligning these dimensions along the model development lifecycle, the taxonomy clarifies how individual architectural or methodological choices contribute to the progression from general pretraining to contextual grounding and real-world application, thereby offering a unifying structure for subsequent analysis in Section 3.
description2: The figure presents a conceptual taxonomy and lifecycle of foundation models for sensor-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR), structured into four phases.
description3: A multi-panel plot organizes foundation model development into four phases: Prepare, Pretraining, Adaptation, and Application, with several sub-categories within each.
questions: What are the main stages involved in developing sensor-based human activity recognition models?
How do different architectural and methodological choices influence model development?

caption: Fig. 7. Application domains for sensor-based HAR foundation models. The radial layout highlights four commonly targeted areas: general-purpose HAR / daily living [58, 119, 145, 157] , healthcare and wellbeing [20, 82, 128, 137, 151], smart-home and context-aware environments [98, 146, 159, 161] , and interactive/agentic assistants [113, 138, 158].
description2: The figure displays a radial layout highlighting four application domains for sensor-based human activity recognition foundation models.
description3: A radial chart organizes application domains—general-purpose HAR, healthcare, smart-home, and interactive assistants—with associated bullet points.
questions: What are the primary application areas for sensor-based human activity recognition?
How are different application areas categorized within the foundation model landscape?
